parsed.title: Electron Theory and Materials
parsed.subject: C&G 2365
parsed.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.
keyIdeas: Electricity is the movement of electrons; understanding their 'home' in the atom is essential.
The nucleus contains protons and neutrons and remains stationary in a conductor.
Electrons are negatively charged and orbit the nucleus in layers called shells.
The outermost shell (valence shell) determines a material's electrical properties.
Conductors have many free electrons that can easily move between atoms.
Insulators have tightly bound electrons that cannot move, preventing current flow.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To establish the atomic foundation of electricity by understanding electron movement and how it defines the properties of conductors and insulators.
keyIdeas: Electricity is the movement of electrons; you cannot understand current without knowing where electrons live.
The arrangement in the outermost shell determines how easily a material conducts.
Atomic structure (protons, neutrons, electrons) is the basis of all electrical science.
anchorFacts: Electricity is the movement of electrons.
Atomic structure consists of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Electron shells and valence electrons determine conductivity.
Free electrons are the charge carriers in a circuit.
Conductors allow current flow; insulators block it.
taskConstraints: Teach atomic structure before defining conductors.
Ensure the distinction between 'free' and 'bound' electrons is clear.
Do not drift into Ohm's Law or Resistivity formulas.

steps: Atomic Structure
Electron Charge and Shells
Free Electrons
Conductors
Insulators

steps.title: Atomic Structure
steps.objective: Identify the three main components of an atom and their locations.
keyIdeas: The nucleus is the stationary center of the atom containing protons and neutrons.
Electrons are the mobile parts that orbit the nucleus.
In a wire, the nucleus stays put; only the electrons move to create current.
anchorFacts: Every material is made of atoms.
The center of the atom is the nucleus.
Electrons orbit the nucleus like planets around a sun.
Electricity is specifically the movement of orbiting electrons.
Nucleus is the name of the central part of an atom that contains protons and neutrons.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking the nucleus moves during electrical flow.
Confusing the nucleus with the entire atom.
taskConstraints: Define the nucleus as stationary.
Define electrons as the mobile charge carriers.
steps.questionIntent: Ensure the learner understands the physical layout of the atom and which part is mobile.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: To confirm the learner understands that the nucleus is locked in the material structure while electrons are mobile.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the central part of an atom that contains protons and neutrons?
Which part of the atom orbits around the center and is responsible for electricity?
In an electrical circuit, does the nucleus of the atom move through the copper wire?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is the movement of electrons, rather than the nucleus, the basis of electrical current in a wire?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The nucleus remains fixed in place
Electrons are the mobile parts of the atom
The nucleus is stationary within the material structure
steps.hint: Think of the nucleus as the 'sun' and the electrons as 'planets' that do all the traveling.

steps.title: Electron Charge and Shells
steps.objective: Explain the negative charge of electrons and their arrangement in shells.
keyIdeas: Electrons carry a negative electrical charge.
Electrons are arranged in layers called shells.
The outermost shell is the only one involved in electrical conduction.
anchorFacts: Electrons have a negative charge; protons have a positive charge.
Electrons inhabit distinct layers called shells.
The electrical properties of a material depend on the outermost shell.
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing electrons have a positive charge.
Thinking all shells are involved in electrical conduction.
taskConstraints: Identify the negative charge of electrons.
Highlight the importance of the outer shell.
steps.questionIntent: Verify knowledge of electron charge and the significance of the outer shell.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: To show that inner shells are 'shielded' or locked, while the outer shell is the interaction point.

basicQuestions.questionText: What type of electrical charge does an electron carry?
What are the layers or orbits that electrons inhabit called?
Which specific shell determines the electrical properties of a material?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the arrangement of electrons in shells explain why only certain parts of an atom are involved in electricity?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Inner shells are not involved in conduction
The outermost shell is the interaction point
Electrical properties are determined by the outer layer only
steps.hint: Electrons are negative; remember that the 'outer' layer is the one that interacts with the world.

steps.title: Free Electrons
steps.objective: Define 'free electrons' and their role in electrical current.
keyIdeas: The outermost shell is called the Valence Shell.
Electrons in the valence shell that are loosely held are 'free electrons'.
Current is the organized drift of these free electrons through a material.
anchorFacts: The valence shell is the outermost shell.
Free electrons can break away from their parent atom.
Electric current is the movement of free electrons.
Materials without free electrons cannot support current flow.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking all electrons in an atom are free to move.
Confusing the valence shell with the nucleus.
taskConstraints: Define 'Valence Shell'.
Define 'Free Electrons' as the mechanism for current.
steps.questionIntent: Confirm the learner understands the specific role of free electrons in current flow.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: To reinforce that 'bound' electrons cannot contribute to current.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the technical name for the outermost shell of an atom?
What do we call electrons that can easily break away and move to neighboring atoms?
What is an electric current defined as at an atomic level?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is a material unable to support an electric current if it lacks free electrons, even if it has many atoms?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Current requires the physical movement of electrons between atoms
Bound electrons cannot break away to create flow
Without free electrons there is no mobile charge carrier
steps.hint: Only the 'free' ones can move; the others are locked in their shells.

steps.title: Conductors
steps.objective: Describe the atomic characteristics of a conductor.
keyIdeas: Conductors have a high number of free electrons.
Copper is the standard choice for cables due to efficiency and cost.
Silver is the best conductor but too expensive for general use.
Aluminum is used for large distribution cables because it is lightweight.
anchorFacts: A conductor has many free electrons.
Copper is the most common cable material.
Silver is a better conductor than copper.
Aluminum is lightweight and used in distribution.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming all metals are equally good conductors.
Thinking copper is the absolute best conductor (Silver is better).
taskConstraints: Link high free electron count to conductivity.
Mention Copper, Silver, and Aluminum.
steps.questionIntent: Verify the learner can identify conductors and their atomic properties.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: To introduce practical trade considerations like cost-effectiveness.

basicQuestions.questionText: Does a conductor have a high or low number of free electrons?
Which metal is the most common material used for standard electrical cables?
Which metal is often used for large distribution cables because it is lightweight?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If silver is a better conductor than copper, why is copper the standard choice for general electrical installations?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Silver is too expensive
Copper is more cost-effective
Copper provides a balance of efficiency and cost
steps.hint: Conductors 'conduct' or lead the way for electrons because they have plenty of 'free' ones.

steps.title: Insulators
steps.objective: Describe the atomic characteristics of an insulator.
keyIdeas: Insulators have atoms that hold onto their electrons very tightly.
Insulators have almost no free electrons.
PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) is the most common insulation for electrical wires.
Insulation is used for safety and to contain current.
anchorFacts: Insulators prevent current flow.
PVC stands for Polyvinyl Chloride.
Common insulators include Rubber, Glass, and PVC.
Safety depends on the integrity of insulating materials.
Polyvinyl Chloride is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking insulators have no electrons at all.
Confusing the conductor with the insulator.
taskConstraints: Define insulators by their lack of free electrons.
Identify PVC as a key material.
steps.questionIntent: Verify the learner understands how insulators work and why they are used.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: To ensure the learner understands that 'bound' electrons cannot move.

basicQuestions.questionText: How tightly do the atoms in an insulator hold their electrons?
What does the abbreviation PVC stand for?
What is the primary purpose of using an insulator around a conductor?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Explain why an insulator does not allow current to flow, even though it is made of atoms containing electrons.
deeperAnswerGuidance: The electrons are not free to move
Electrons are tightly bound to the nucleus
There are almost no free electrons available
steps.hint: Insulators 'insulate' or isolate the electricity; they keep the electrons from escaping.

steps.title: Worked Example: Classifying Materials
steps.objective: Classify a material as a conductor or insulator by analyzing its electron availability.
keyIdeas: Analyzing a material based on its atomic structure to determine its use in the trade.
anchorFacts: Copper has one loosely held valence electron.
PVC has tightly bound valence electrons.
taskConstraints: Use a real-world cable scenario.
Focus on electron mobility.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are examining a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You need to explain why the inner core is copper and the outer coating is PVC.
steps: Analyze the Copper core: It has a single electron in its valence shell that is loosely held.
Identify 'Free Electrons': Because that electron is loose, it can break away and jump to neighboring atoms.
Analyze the PVC: The atoms hold their electrons tightly to the nucleus.
Determine mobility: Because PVC electrons are locked, no organized drift (current) can occur.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: The difference is the 'freedom' of the electrons to move, not the presence of electrons themselves.

steps.hint: Focus on the valence shell; if the electrons can jump from atom to atom, you have a conductor.

keyIdeas: Applying electron theory to overhead power line materials.

Ceramic has tightly bound electrons.
taskConstraints: Provide a concrete scenario.
Ask for a specific classification.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are looking at an overhead power line made of Aluminum supported by ceramic insulators.
steps: Aluminum has three electrons in its valence shell that are loosely held.
Ceramic atoms hold their electrons very tightly to the nucleus.
Determine which material allows the electrons to move from atom to atom.

steps.questionText: Based on the fact that Aluminum has loosely held electrons that can move between atoms, is it classified as a conductor or an insulator?
steps.hint: Think about whether the electrons are 'free' to move or 'trapped' in place.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Verify understanding of basic electron theory and the fundamental differences between conductive and insulating materials.
keyIdeas: Identifying the specific mechanism of current flow.
anchorFacts: Current is the organized drift of free electrons.
taskConstraints: Use a specific technical question.
Avoid vague prompts.
steps.questionIntent: To ensure the learner knows the exact name of the particles that move.

taskSkeleton.scenario: In electrical science, we categorize materials based on how their atoms hold onto their electrons.
steps: Conductors allow for an 'organized drift' of particles, while insulators prevent this movement.

steps.questionText: What is the specific name for the particles in a conductor that break away from their atoms to create an organized drift?
steps.hint: These are the 'commuters' of the atomic world that jump from one atom to another.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize electron theory with practical material selection by explaining the atomic difference between conductors and insulators.
keyIdeas: Connecting atomic structure to real-world cable components.
anchorFacts: Copper is a conductor; PVC is an insulator.
taskConstraints: Require a structured response addressing three specific points.
Ensure marking points are discrete.
steps.questionIntent: To synthesize the entire lesson into a practical explanation of a standard cable.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A standard electrical cable consists of a copper internal core and a PVC outer sheath.
steps: Explain the difference between the copper core and PVC sheath by addressing: 1) The state of electrons in copper, 2) The state of electrons in PVC, and 3) What 'current' actually is at an atomic level.
requiredOutputs: Current is the organized drift of free electrons

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why PVC electrons are tightly bound is the correct wiring system or cable type for this Copper has many free electrons scenario.
steps.hint: Focus on the 'mobility' of the electrons in the outermost shell of each material.

- teachingBeforeTesting: 0-25
- markingRobustness: 0-20
- alignmentToLO: 0-15
- questionQuality: 0-10

Strict rules:
- total must equal the breakdown sum.
- If content is generic, placeholder-like, or structurally weak, score harshly.
- If validationIssues is non-empty, grade must be rework.
- A score of 100 is extremely rare and should only be used if there are genuinely no meaningful pedagogical weaknesses.
- If you can name even one real weakness, do not return 100 and do not return an empty issues array.
- Penalize bundled or multi-part tasks when the answer guidance does not map clearly to each required part.
- Penalize bundled tasks most strongly when one response field mixes different output types, multiple scenarios, or several labeled requirements that would be hard to mark independently.
- Do not over-penalize simple same-type recall lists if the answer guidance maps cleanly; focus on genuinely hard-to-mark mixed-output tasks.
- If a task bundles Scenario A and Scenario B with different labels or answer types into one answer box, prioritise that bundled-task defect above secondary wording issues.
- Penalize open-ended wording that is hard to mark reliably from learner phrasing.
- Penalize any drift into out-of-scope content, even if the lesson is otherwise strong.
- If a task expects a specific technical term, penalize answer guidance that accepts vague generic wording.
- Penalize answer guidance that includes factually wrong answers, category labels instead of the asked term, or answers that belong to a different question in the same step.
- Treat contaminated answer guidance as a high-impact markingRobustness problem, especially when a single-question answer list contains material copied from neighbouring questions.
- If a question asks for one code letter, one abbreviation expansion, one exact term, or one named item, the guidance should prioritise only that target rather than nearby concepts or topic labels.
- For teach_check steps, treat basicQuestions as the authoritative question structure. Do not penalize the derived combined questionText field if the underlying basicQuestions are sound.
- phaseFeedback must cover every lesson step exactly once, in lesson order.
- Every phaseFeedback item must include at least one concrete strength or one concrete issue.
- summary must be 2-3 sentences maximum.

Grounded source text:
Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO4 - Resistance, resistivity, voltage, current, power

Lesson: 202-4A - Electron Theory and Materials

Topic focus: Electron Theory and Materials

Prerequisites: 202-3B

Must-have topics: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO4.AC4.1: Basic electron theory
- 202.LO4.AC4.2: Conductors vs insulators

Lesson split rationale: Establishes the atomic basis of electricity and how it determines the properties of conductors and insulators.

In-scope teaching anchors: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Out-of-scope topics: Resistance, Resistivity, and Voltage Drop; DC Circuit Relationships and Calculations; Power and Effects of Electric Current

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (1bae6c6b-9616-4001-a615-4d6a7980507b): ## LO4 — Resistance, resistivity, voltage, current, power **AC** 1. Basic electron theory 2. Conductors vs insulators 3. Meaning of resistance and resistivity (in circuits) 4. Relationship between current/voltage/resistance in series & parallel D.C. circuits 5. Calculate current/voltage/resistance in series & parallel D.C. circuits 6. Calculate power in series & parallel D.C. circuits 7. Meaning of voltage drop 8. Chemical and thermal effects of electric currents ---

Vocabulary output:
{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "Atom",
      "simpleDefinition": "The smallest building block of matter, consisting of a nucleus and orbiting electrons.",
      "anchor": "The 'solar system' of electricity where the nucleus is the sun and electrons are the planets.",
      "misconception": "Thinking atoms are solid, unbreakable balls rather than mostly empty space with moving parts."
    },
    {
      "term": "Nucleus",
      "simpleDefinition": "The central part of an atom containing protons and neutrons; it holds most of the atom's mass.",
      "anchor": "The 'engine room' of the atom that stays fixed in place while electricity happens around it.",
      "misconception": "Believing the nucleus moves through a wire when current flows."
    },
    {
      "term": "Electron",
      "simpleDefinition": "A tiny, negatively charged particle that orbits the nucleus of an atom.",
      "anchor": "The 'fuel' of electricity; their movement is what we measure as current.",
      "misconception": "Confusing electrons with protons and thinking they have a positive charge."
    },
    {
      "term": "Free Electron",
      "simpleDefinition": "An electron in the outermost shell that can easily break away and move to a neighboring atom.",
      "anchor": "A 'commuter' jumping from one train carriage to another.",
      "misconception": "Thinking every electron in an atom is free to move and contribute to current."
    },
    {
      "term": "Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "A material, usually a metal, that has many free electrons and allows electricity to flow easily.",
      "anchor": "A wide-open motorway that lets electron traffic pass without a struggle.",
      "misconception": "Assuming all metals are equally good at conducting electricity."
    },
    {
      "term": "Insulator",
      "simpleDefinition": "A material with atoms that hold onto their electrons tightly, preventing the flow of electricity.",
      "anchor": "A high security fence that keeps electrons locked in place.",
      "misconception": "Thinking that insulators have no electrons at all, rather than just 'stuck' ones."
    },
    {
      "term": "Valence Shell",
      "simpleDefinition": "The outermost layer or orbit of an atom where the free electrons live.",
      "anchor": "The 'exit door' of the atom.",
      "misconception": "Thinking that internal shells are involved in the day-to-day flow of electricity."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "Electricity is the flow of free electrons.",
    "The nucleus stays still; only electrons move.",
    "Conductors have plenty of free electrons; insulators have almost none.",
    "The outer shell determines if a material conducts."
  ],
  "misconceptionTargets": [
    "Thinking the nucleus moves during electrical flow.",
    "Believing electrons have a positive charge.",
    "Thinking all electrons in an atom are free to move.",
    "Assuming insulators contain no electrons."
  ]
}

issues.excerpt: "questionText": "What is the name of the central part of an atom?", "answerGuidance": ["Atom"]
issues.problem: The answer guidance is factually incorrect. The central part of an atom is the nucleus, not the 'Atom' itself.
issues.whyItMatters: Learners who provide the correct technical term (Nucleus) will be marked incorrect, while those who repeat the subject of the question will be marked correct. This destroys the pedagogical value of the check.
issues.suggestion: Change the answer guidance for the first question in Step 2 to 'Nucleus'.

issues.excerpt: From the scenario, identify the specific technical answer that best fits.
issues.problem: The question is extremely vague and relies on the learner guessing which specific term from the scenario the tutor wants.
issues.whyItMatters: Vague prompts lead to frustration and high failure rates in automated marking, as the learner doesn't know if they should answer 'Conductors', 'Organized drift', or 'Free electrons'.
issues.suggestion: Rewrite the question to be specific: 'What is the name of the particles that create an organized drift in a conductor?'

issues.excerpt: Explain the difference in atomic behavior that allows the copper core of a cable to conduct electricity while the PVC sheath acts as an insulator.
issues.problem: The task bundles four distinct conceptual requirements into a single open-ended response field.
issues.whyItMatters: It is difficult to reliably mark a single paragraph against four specific criteria (Copper valence, PVC binding, current definition, insulator lack of free electrons) without a structured input or very sophisticated NLP.
issues.suggestion: Break the integrative close into two or three smaller, targeted questions or use a bulleted list requirement.
